key_points: Systematic review following PRISMA 2020 guidelines; 15 articles included from 2015-2025 on Er:YAG laser-assisted root canal disinfection.
All 15 studies found Er:YAG laser activation significantly improved root canal disinfection compared to conventional or ultrasonic methods.
PIPS (Photon-Induced Photoacoustic Streaming) and SWEEPS (Shock Wave-Enhanced Emission Photoacoustic Streaming) techniques yielded superior bacterial reduction, especially in the apical region.
Er:YAG laser activation enabled use of lower sodium hypochlorite concentrations without sacrificing disinfection efficacy.
Er:YAG's strong water absorption generates photoacoustic streaming, improving smear layer removal with lower thermal risk compared to other laser systems.
Some studies indicated reduced post-operative discomfort with Er:YAG laser-assisted irrigation.
Significant heterogeneity in protocols, laser parameters, and outcome measures across studies limits direct comparisons.
Authors call for standardized, long-term clinical trials to solidify guidelines and confirm long-term clinical benefits.
Er:YAG laser-assisted irrigation supports deeper irrigant penetration into complex canal anatomies.

outcomes.result: All included studies reported significant improvement in root canal disinfection with Er:YAG laser over conventional or ultrasonic methods; PIPS and SWEEPS showed superior apical bacterial reduction.
outcomes.clinical_significance: Improved disinfection especially in apical third where conventional irrigation is limited.

outcomes.result: Er:YAG laser-assisted irrigation improved smear layer removal compared to conventional methods.
outcomes.clinical_significance: Better smear layer removal supports deeper irrigant penetration into dentinal tubules.

outcomes.result: Er:YAG laser activation effectively disrupted endodontic biofilms.
outcomes.clinical_significance: Biofilm disruption is critical for successful endodontic treatment outcomes.

outcomes.result: Some studies indicated reduced post-operative discomfort with Er:YAG laser-assisted irrigation.
outcomes.clinical_significance: Potential patient comfort benefit, though not consistently reported across all studies.

outcomes.result: PIPS and SWEEPS techniques allowed use of lower sodium hypochlorite concentrations without sacrificing efficacy.
outcomes.clinical_significance: Lower NaOCl concentrations may reduce risk of periapical tissue damage.

notes: Risk of bias assessed using a ten-item checklist based on Cochrane Handbook guidelines. Heterogeneity in laser parameters across included studies is a major limitation. Only studies from 2015-2025 were included.

population.sample_type: Mixed: in vitro, ex vivo, and clinical studies included in systematic review

superiority_claim: El autor afirma que PIPS y SWEEPS son superiores a los métodos convencionales y ultrasónicos para la reducción bacteriana apical en conductos radiculares: 'Photon-induced photoacoustic streaming (PIPS) and shock wave-enhanced emission photoacoustic streaming (SWEEPS) yielded superior bacterial reduction, especially apically.'
source_citation: Fiegler-Rudol, J., Grzech-Leśniak, Z., Tkaczyk, M., Grzech-Leśniak, K., Zawilska, A., & Wiench, R. (2025). Enhancing Root Canal Disinfection with Er:YAG Laser: A Systematic Review. Dentistry Journal, 13(3), 101. https://doi.org/10.3390/dj13030101

treatment_protocol.application_technique: PIPS (Photon-Induced Photoacoustic Streaming) and SWEEPS (Shock Wave-Enhanced Emission Photoacoustic Streaming)

clinical_indication: Root canal disinfection, biofilm disruption, smear layer removal in endodontic treatment
target_tissue: dentin, root canal walls, endodontic biofilm

drugs_mentioned.name: sodium hypochlorite
drugs_mentioned.dental_relevance: Standard endodontic irrigant; Er:YAG laser activation (PIPS/SWEEPS) allows use of lower concentrations without sacrificing disinfection efficacy, potentially reducing risk of periapical tissue damage.
drugs_mentioned.laser_implication: Er:YAG laser photoacoustic activation enhances efficacy of sodium hypochlorite, enabling lower concentrations to be used during root canal irrigation.

uncertainties: Specific laser parameters (power, fluence, pulse duration, frequency, spot size) from individual included studies were not reported in the abstract; only the Er:YAG wavelength (2940 nm) is implied by the laser type.
The exact number and types of study designs included (in vitro vs. clinical) are not specified in the abstract.
Conflicts of interest and funding sources are not declared in the available abstract.
Risk of bias assessment results for individual studies are not detailed in the abstract.
Long-term clinical outcomes data are acknowledged as lacking by the authors themselves.
